title: The Ontology of Coexistence
description: Madhyasth Darshan's account of existence as the coexistence of satta and units — saturation, the four orders, sentience, causation, conservation, and the reality of the world — compared in depth with Advaita Vedanta and modern physics and philosophy

title: Nature of Time
description: What time (kaal) is: the duration of unit-activity against timeless satta, numerical reckoning, and the past–present–future structure — compared with Advaita Vedanta, the philosophy of time, and physics (spacetime, cosmology, entropy)

title: Why Humans Are Not Just Material
description: Whether a human being is only a physical–chemical body: Madhyasth Darshan's jeevan-and-body account set against mainstream science and Advaita Vedanta, with a critical review of all three

title: Philosophy of Mind and Jeevan
description: How Madhyasth Darshan understands the inner life: jeevan and its faculties — mun, vritti, chitta, buddhi, and atma — and desire, imagination, selection, delusion, and awakening

title: Knowledge Knower and Known
description: What knowledge is, who the knower is, and what can be known: the four senses of gyan, the staged path to knowing, and conduct as evidence — compared with Advaita Vedanta and modern epistemology and science

title: Methodology and Hermeneutics
description: The method behind these studies: reading the primary texts, translation issues, citation discipline, interpretation, and disciplined comparison across traditions

title: Axiology Value Theory
description: Madhyasth Darshan's theory of value: what values are, how they are recognised and fulfilled, and how mutual satisfaction grounds a humane axiology

title: Ethics and Morals in Human Beings
description: Whether ethics is divine command, convention, evolution, reason, or evidence of understanding: Madhyasth Darshan's account of humane conduct through justice, dharma, truth, and right-use, compared with Advaita Vedanta and modern moral science

title: Family Relationships and Values
description: The family as the first site of values: recognising relationships and living trust, affection, gratitude, and mutual satisfaction

title: Education and Sanskar
description: How understanding is formed and carried forward: education, sanskar, study, awakening, and transmission across generations

title: Aesthetics
description: What beauty and art are: Madhyasth Darshan's functional aesthetics of usefulness, harmony, and human purpose, compared with modern philosophical aesthetics and Advaita Vedanta

title: Human Behavior and Society
description: How humans should behave and organise: Madhyasth Darshan on conduct, ethics, and social order, compared with the natural sciences, Advaita Vedanta, and religious and technocratic approaches

title: How to Form Self-Sustaining Organizations
description: Why organisations built on fear or accumulation must extract from their members and dissolve, and how Madhyasth Darshan grounds organisations that sustain themselves without instrumentalising the people in them

title: Governance Justice and Undivided Society
description: Justice, governance, and law in Madhyasth Darshan: fearlessness, public order, and the institutions of an undivided society

title: Prosperity Economics and Right Use
description: Prosperity without accumulation: production, cyclical economics, right-use of wealth, and the limits of accumulation

title: Nature Ecology and Right Use
description: Living with the other orders of nature: Earth, right-use, regeneration, and ecological balance within coexistence

title: Spiritual Practice and Realization
description: Realization and awakening in Madhyasth Darshan: anubhav, practice, yoga, and liberation from mystery and suffering, compared with moksha traditions

title: Science Technology and Human Purpose
description: Science, technology, and human purpose: production and convenience, and the limits of instrumental knowledge

title: How Undivided Society Is Established
description: Madhyasth Darshan's vision of undivided society (akhand samaj) and universal orderliness: the full architecture from coexistence through individual awakening to social evidence, drawn from the primary texts

title: Death Continuity and Rebirth
description: What happens at death in Madhyasth Darshan: the continuity of jeevan beyond the body and its re-embodiment, compared with Advaita Vedanta's rebirth and the physicalist view that death ends the self

title: Language Meaning and Definition
description: Madhyasth Darshan's claim that reality is fully expressible: word, meaning, and definition (shabd, artha, Paribhasha), set against Advaita Vedanta's ineffability and modern philosophy of language

title: Work Action and Karma
description: Human action and work in Madhyasth Darshan: effort-motion-result (shram-gati-parinam), karma, labour, and right-use, and how action carries consequence

title: Free Will Choice and Agency
description: Whether human conduct is free or determined: definite conduct in the lower orders versus achieved selection at the knowledge order, compared with debates on free will and determinism

title: Health Body and Restraint
description: The jeevan-body relationship in health: ahar-vihar, restraint (sanyam), and well-being as the health-restraint dimension of universal orderliness

title: God Divinity and the Sacred
description: How Madhyasth Darshan reframes God and the divine: satta as omnipresence and the awakened (divya) human, compared with theism, Advaita Vedanta's Ishvara, and secular critiques of religion
